did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized a mobile application based on artificial intelligence for population screening of skin tumors. The AI method involved an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for image quality control, segmentation (using a CNN model to detect lesion boundaries), and classification (using a pre-trained model fine-tuned via Google's Vertex AI to classify lesions into benign and malignant classes such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma). The study processed over 500,000 images from users to identify suspicious cases of skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a population screening study using a mobile AI application ('ProRodinki'). It details the methodology (CNN architecture, Google Vertex AI), data collection (over 500,000 images from 290,000+ users), and specific clinical outcomes (identification of 5,957 non-melanoma and 7,622 melanoma suspicious cases). Although the section header is 'Lectures for doctors' (Лекции для врачей), the content is a primary research report with a Material and Methods, Results, and Conclusion structure, rather than a narrative review of existing literature.

title: Искусственный интеллект как инструмент популяционного скрининга опухолей кожи
brief_summary: This study evaluated the feasibility of using the 'ProRodinki' mobile application, powered by an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Google Vertex AI, for population screening of skin tumors. The AI system processed over 500,000 smartphone images from 290,000 users across Russia to detect and classify lesions into benign or malignant categories, specifically targeting melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancers. The model utilized CNNs for image quality control, lesion segmentation, and classification, achieving high accuracy and sensitivity on test sets. During the study period, the application identified 5,957 suspicious cases of non-melanoma skin cancer and 7,622 suspicious cases of melanoma. The results indicated a significantly higher prevalence of skin cancer and a 15-year younger average age of patients compared to official statistics. The authors concluded that this AI-based tool is an effective, low-cost method for raising public awareness and facilitating early detection of skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer; one of the primary classes in the 5-class classification model and a key focus of the screening results (7,622 suspicious cases reported in abstract, 5,230 in results section).
Malignant skin cancer; grouped with SCC in the classification model as 'БКРК/ПКРК'. Reported as 5,957 suspicious cases of non-melanoma skin cancer in the abstract.
Malignant skin cancer; grouped with BCC in the classification model as 'БКРК/ПКРК'. Reported as part of the 5,957 suspicious cases of non-melanoma skin cancer in the abstract.
Benign lesion; one of the five classes in the CNN classification model. Confusion matrix shows 82% accuracy for correct classification.
Benign lesion; one of the five classes in the CNN classification model. Confusion matrix shows 96% accuracy for correct classification.
Benign lesion; one of the five classes in the CNN classification model. Confusion matrix shows 88% accuracy for correct classification.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant risk) and specific lesion classes (nevus, hemangioma, seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, BCC/SCC).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a CNN-based ensemble that performs binary classification (high/low risk of skin cancer) for user feedback and multiclass classification (5 classes: nevus, hemangioma, seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, BCC/SCC) for internal model evaluation. The model uses Google Vertex AI and achieves 90.92% accuracy and 86.94% sensitivity on a test set of 1,233 images. The primary output provided to users is a binary risk score (high/low) to prompt medical consultation.
A CNN detection model is used to identify lesion boundaries and crop the image for classification. It achieved 91.94% accuracy and 81.06% sensitivity on a test set of 1,523 images.

datasets_used.name: ProRodinki Application Dataset
datasets_used.description: Images and metadata collected from users of the 'ProRodinki' mobile application for Android/iOS between January 2021 and December 2022. The dataset includes smartphone photographs of skin lesions, demographic information, and risk factors from users across 86 regions of the Russian Federation.
classes_used: Malignant: Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)

use.training: Used for population screening; images were processed by the AI model. Specific training counts for this live dataset are not reported as it was used for inference/screening.
use.other: Used for population screening to identify suspicious cases (5,957 non-melanoma and 7,622 melanoma suspicious cases reported in abstract; 9,321 total suspicious cases in results section).

datasets_used.name: Clinical Image Dataset for Model Development
datasets_used.description: A curated set of verified clinical images used to train and evaluate the CNN detection and classification models. This dataset was collected by specialists from the Privolzhsky Research Medical University (PRMU) and the University Clinic.

use.training: 7,680 verified clinical images collected by PRMU specialists were used to fine-tune the Google Vertex AI model for lesion detection. An additional 3,749 images collected via the app in early 2021 and classified by University Clinic dermatologists were added to the dataset for classification model training.
use.testing: 1,523 images were used to evaluate the detection model (accuracy 91.94%, sensitivity 81.06%). 1,233 images were used to evaluate the classification model (accuracy 90.92%, sensitivity 86.94%).
use.other: Used for model development, fine-tuning, and performance evaluation.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image database used for pre-training the convolutional neural networks before fine-tuning for the specific dermatology task.

use.training: Used as the basis for pre-trained models to leverage learned features for image classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the CNN classification model by calculating accuracy and sensitivity for each of the five lesion classes.
To analyze the demographic profile of users (age distribution) and the age of patients with suspicious lesions, comparing them to official statistics.
To estimate the prevalence of non-melanoma skin cancer and melanoma among the screening population and compare it with official statistics.
To filter out low-quality images before analysis using specific statistical thresholds for illumination (local contrast based on standard deviation) and sharpness (Laplacian filter).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, chi-square tests). It reported descriptive statistics (means, medians, quartiles) and prevalence rates, noting that the average age of patients was 'significantly younger' and prevalence was 'significantly higher' compared to official statistics, but did not provide statistical significance values or specify a testing method.

Clinical Image Dataset for Model Develop.description: Curated set of verified clinical images used to train and evaluate the CNN detection and classification models. Total size: 11,429 images.
Clinical Image Dataset for Model Develop.notes: Confusion matrix results for classification model (Fig. 2B/2C): Nevus (82% accuracy), Hemangioma (96% accuracy), BCC/SCC (93% accuracy), Seborrheic Keratosis (88% accuracy), Melanoma (88% accuracy).

ProRodinki Application Dataset.description: Images and metadata collected from users of the 'ProRodinki' mobile application between January 2021 and December 2022. Total images processed: >500,000 from >290,000 users across 86 regions of the Russian Federation.
ProRodinki Application Dataset.notes: The study reports that the average age of patients with skin cancer identified via the app was significantly younger (by ~15 years) compared to official Russian statistics (official average age for melanoma: 61.5 years for men, 62.1 years for women; for non-melanoma: 68.2 years for men, 70.2 years for women).

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for detection and classification, utilizing Google Vertex AI for fine-tuning pre-trained models.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models from ImageNet were used as the base for both the detection and classification CNNs before fine-tuning on clinical datasets.
architecture_used.input_size: Minimum image size of 100 pixels; specific input dimensions for the neural networks are not reported.

detection_model.function: Lesion boundary detection and cropping (Bounding Box generation).
detection_model.base: Pre-trained Google model fine-tuned via Vertex AI.
detection_model.training_data: 7,680 verified clinical images collected by Privolzhsky Research Medical University (PRMU).
detection_model.quality_control: OpenCV library used for image quality checks: local contrast threshold (std dev) of 63, sharpness threshold (Laplacian filter) of 16, minimum size 100 pixels.

classification_model.function: Multi-class classification into 5 classes (Nevus, Hemangioma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Melanoma, BCC/SCC) and binary risk assessment.
classification_model.base: Pre-trained Google model fine-tuned via Vertex AI.
classification_model.training_data: 11,429 clinical images (7,680 from detection dataset + 3,749 from app users classified by University Clinic dermatologists).
classification_model.output_head: SoftMax layer for multi-class probabilities; binary output for user-facing risk assessment (high/low risk).

classification_or_output_head.detection_output: Bounding Box (BBox) coordinates for lesion cropping.
classification_or_output_head.classification_output: SoftMax probabilities for 5 classes; binary high/low risk score for clinical decision support.

preprocessing_techniques: Image quality control using OpenCV library with specific thresholds: local contrast based on standard deviation (threshold 63), sharpness using Laplacian filter (threshold 16), and minimum image size (100 pixels).
Rejection of images failing quality checks (approx. 37% rejection rate).
CNN-based lesion boundary detection and cropping (bounding box generation) to isolate the lesion for classification.
Use of pre-trained models from ImageNet as a base for transfer learning.
conclusions: The mobile application 'ProRodinki' based on artificial intelligence is a feasible and effective tool for population screening of skin tumors, enabling remote collection and processing of data without significant financial investment from the state healthcare system.

The AI system successfully processed over 500,000 images from more than 290,000 users across 86 regions of the Russian Federation, identifying 5,957 suspicious cases of non-melanoma skin cancer and 7,622 suspicious cases of melanoma.

The study revealed a significantly higher prevalence of skin cancer per 100,000 population compared to official statistics, indicating that traditional screening programs may underestimate disease burden.

The average age of patients with suspicious skin cancer lesions identified via the AI tool was approximately 15 years younger than reported in official Russian statistics, suggesting that AI-based screening can detect skin cancer at earlier stages in younger populations.

The AI-based self-examination tool can effectively raise public awareness about skin cancer risk factors and facilitate the routing of users with suspicious lesions to specialized medical institutions for timely consultation.

The CNN-based ensemble model achieved high performance metrics, with 90.92% accuracy and 86.94% sensitivity for classification, and 91.94% accuracy and 81.06% sensitivity for detection, demonstrating the technical viability of smartphone-based AI dermatology tools.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the AI model was trained and tested on a curated clinical dataset from Privolzhsky Research Medical University and the University Clinic, not on the live population screening data.

There is a significant risk of data leakage and selection bias: the training dataset for the classification model included 3,749 images collected via the app in early 2021 that were retrospectively classified by dermatologists, potentially inflating performance metrics compared to real-world inference.

The results rely heavily on self-reported metadata (e.g., phototype, family history, number of nevi) which the authors acknowledge may contain subjective distortions; the paper states that 'the sum of collected data evens out consciously distorted answers' without statistical proof of this claim.
No informed consent was obtained from the 290,000+ users, as the authors declare 'Not applicable' for informed consent, raising ethical concerns regarding the use of user-generated health data.

The study does not report ground-truth histopathological confirmation for the 9,321 suspicious cases identified in the population screening; diagnoses are based solely on AI predictions and user-reported risk factors.

The prevalence rates reported (e.g., 11.88 per 100,000 for melanoma in women) are derived from a self-selected, app-using population and cannot be generalized to the general Russian population without accounting for significant selection bias.

The paper does not report sensitivity/specificity trade-offs or false positive rates for the population screening phase, only accuracy and sensitivity on the small, curated test sets (n=1,233 for classification, n=1,523 for detection).
The model architecture details are limited; while it uses Google Vertex AI and pre-trained ImageNet models, specific hyperparameters, loss functions, and optimizer details are not reported.

The study acknowledges that the average age of detected cancer patients was 15 years younger than official statistics, but attributes this partly to 'insufficient coverage of the older generation,' indicating a demographic bias in the screening population.
